title: Distributed Caching Architecture
description: Caching is the single most impactful performance optimization in distributed systems. This file covers caching patterns (cache-aside, write-through, write-behind, refresh-ahead), cache invalidation strategies, distributed cache topologies (Redis, Memcached, CDN), cache consistency tradeoffs, and how SAP's table buffering and shared memory patterns translate to distributed caching at FAANG scale.

sections.title: Caching Patterns — When to Use Each

description.value:
There are four primary caching patterns, each with different consistency guarantees and operational characteristics. The Staff+ engineer selects the pattern that matches the data's consistency requirements and access patterns.

1. Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading): Application checks cache first. On cache miss, it reads from the database, populates the cache, and returns. The cache is populated on demand. Pros: efficient (only caches what's requested), resilient to cache failures (fallback to DB). Cons: cache miss adds latency (read-through penalty), stale data until TTL expires or entry is invalidated. Used by: most web applications, SAP table buffering (generic/transactional). SAP analog: SAP's generic table buffering — first read loads the buffer; subsequent reads hit the buffer until the sync point.

2. Write-Through: Application writes to cache, cache synchronously writes to database. Both write paths must succeed. Pros: cache is always consistent with DB (no staleness), reads are always cache hits. Cons: every write must update both cache and DB (higher write latency), cache is always full (memory pressure). Used by: systems where read consistency is critical (session stores, config caches). SAP analog: SAP's single-record buffering updates the buffer on every write — the buffer is always current.

3. Write-Behind (Write-Back): Application writes to cache, cache asynchronously persists to database. Pros: very low write latency (fast acknowledgment), can batch writes to the DB. Cons: data loss risk if cache fails before persistence (unless backed by AOF/RDB in Redis), complex reconciliation if DB write fails. Used by: high-throughput write systems (analytics pipelines, metrics ingestion). SAP analog: V1/V3 update bundling — updates are collected and written to the database asynchronously.

4. Refresh-Ahead: Cache automatically refreshes an entry before it expires (predictive loading). Pros: eliminates cache miss penalty for predictable access patterns. Cons: complex to implement, can waste resources refreshing unused entries. Used by: ML model serving, recommendation caches. SAP analog: SAP's table buffering with refresh-on-expiry (single-record buffering refreshes based on timestamps).

Single-record buf = Write-Through; V3 updates = Write-Behind</text></svg>

"There are only two hard things in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things." — Phil Karlton. Cache invalidation is hard because the cache and database can become inconsistent in ways that are silent, transient, and difficult to debug.

Invalidation strategies ranked by consistency strength:

1. TTL-based expiry (weakest, most common): Cache entries expire after a fixed duration. Simple to implement, no coordination needed between cache and DB. But data can be stale for up to the TTL duration. When to use: data that changes slowly (reference data, user profiles, product catalogs). SAP analog: SAP's table buffering TTL (generic buffering refreshes at program startup or after 2 hours for sync).

2. Write-through invalidation (strong): On every data mutation, the cache entry is updated or deleted synchronously. Requires the write path to always interact with the cache. When to use: data that requires read-after-write consistency (session data, financial balances). SAP analog: SAP's single-record buffering — when a record is updated, the buffer entry is refreshed immediately.

3. Cache-aside with explicit invalidation (moderate): The application explicitly invalidates cache entries when data changes. The next read re-populates the cache. When to use: when the write path knows which cache keys to invalidate. Risk: race condition — one thread invalidates while another thread re-populates with the old value. Solution: use distributed locks or compare-and-swap during re-population.

4. Pub/sub invalidation (event-driven): The database publishes change events (CDC via Kafka/Debezium), and cache consumers invalidate or refresh entries in real-time. When to use: when the write path cannot know all cache consumers (multi-service architectures). SAP analog: SAP's table buffering refresh via RFC — when data changes in one application server, it broadcasts a 'refresh buffer' RFC to all other servers.

5. Version-based (strongest, hardest): Every cache entry carries a version number. The client includes the version in reads; if stale, the cache is invalidated and re-fetched. When to use: when staleness is unacceptable and the read path can tolerate version comparison overhead.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I invalidate cache entries when the database changes?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How much staleness can your application tolerate?" If the answer is 'zero', you need transactional cache coherency (write-through or version-based). If the answer is 'a few seconds', TTL or pub/sub invalidation is sufficient. If the answer is 'minutes to hours', TTL alone is the simplest.

branches.path: Use write-through caching with synchronous cache update on every write. The cache and DB must be updated in the same transaction. Use Redis with AOF persistence for crash recovery. Accept higher write latency (2x-3x).

branches.condition: Seconds of staleness acceptable, complex write path
branches.path: Use pub/sub invalidation via CDC (Debezium → Kafka → cache consumer). The cache is invalidated within milliseconds of the DB commit. More complex infrastructure but fully decoupled — the write path doesn't need to know about the cache.

branches.condition: Minutes of staleness acceptable, simple read-heavy workload
branches.path: Use TTL-based expiry with cache-aside. Set TTL to 60-300 seconds depending on business requirements. This is the simplest and most robust approach. For emergency invalidation, provide an admin API to flush specific cache keys.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ answer to cache invalidation is never a single strategy — it's a hybrid. Use TTL as the default (simple), write-through for critical data (session, auth), and CDC-based invalidation for complex multi-service topologies.

sections.title: Redis vs Memcached — Distributed Cache Topologies

description.value:
Redis and Memcached are the two dominant in-memory cache systems at FAANG scale. They differ fundamentally in data structures, persistence, and clustering capabilities.

Memcached: Simple key-value store. Multi-threaded (uses multiple CPU cores). No persistence (pure cache). No clustering (client-side sharding). Maximum value size: 1MB. Best for: simple key-value caching where data loss is acceptable (session data, HTML fragments, API responses). At Facebook, Memcached is used at a massive scale with client-side consistent hashing and a moxi proxy layer.

Redis: Advanced data structures (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, bitmaps, hyperloglogs, streams). Single-threaded for commands (I/O is multi-threaded). Persistence via RDB (snapshot) or AOF (append-only log). Clustering via Redis Cluster (hash slots, automatic failover). Sentinel for HA. Best for: caching + data structure operations (leaderboards, counters, rate limiters, message queues, session stores). At Twitter, Redis stores timelines and rate limits. At GitHub, Redis caches Git repository metadata.

Redis Cluster topology: 16384 hash slots distributed across nodes. Each key is assigned to a slot via CRC16. Clients can use smart clients (redirect-based) or proxies (Twemproxy, Redis Cluster Proxy). Slots are migrated between nodes for resharding. Failure detection via PING/PONG gossip — nodes mark peers as PFAIL (possibly failed) after N/2 timeouts, and FAIL after cluster-wide agreement.

Consistent hashing (alternative to Redis Cluster): Each cache key is hashed to a position on a ring. Each cache node is assigned to one or more positions. When a node fails, only the keys assigned to that node are re-distributed (partial redistribution). Used by: Memcached clients, Amazon DynamoDB, Cassandra. The tradeoff: client-side logic is more complex, but no central coordination is needed.

Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) cache static and dynamic content at edge locations close to users. CDN caching is a different category from application caching — it operates at the HTTP layer and requires different design patterns.

Static content caching: CSS, JS, images, fonts, media files are cached at the CDN edge. Cache keys are URL-based. Cache-control headers (max-age, s-maxage, stale-while-revalidate) determine TTL. Invalidation: purge by URL or cache tag. FAANG pattern: static assets are served from CDN with long TTLs (1 year) and content-addressable URLs (webpack content hashes, /static/hash/filename.js). When the file changes, the URL changes, so there is no invalidation needed.

Dynamic content caching: API responses, HTML pages, and personalized content can also be cached at the CDN edge. Techniques: (1) Edge-side includes (ESI) — the CDN assembles the page from cached fragments, with personalized fragments fetched from origin. (2) Surrogate keys — the origin tells the CDN which cache tags to associate with a response. Purge by tag invalidates all related content. (3) Stale-while-revalidate — the CDN serves stale content while asynchronously refreshing. Used by Google for fast search result serving.

SAP Bridge: SAP Gateway caching (OData $expand responses, SICF service caching) follows similar patterns. The ETag mechanism in OData is equivalent to CDN cache validation (If-None-Match). SAP BTP's App Router acts as a reverse proxy cache — similar to a CDN edge node for Fiori apps.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Discussing caching in a system design interview is a signal of seniority. Junior engineers mention 'add a cache.' Senior engineers discuss which caching pattern to use. Staff+ engineers lead with cache invalidation ('the hard part is keeping caches consistent') and quantify tradeoffs (memory vs. staleness vs. write latency). Always start with: 'What's our consistency requirement? That determines the caching strategy.'
